dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for unconscious patient, call at 19:44.
Hi, my name is Alex, and I'm an EMT with the VCU Rescue Squad.
I'm Michael, the paramedic. Can you tell us what happened before we arrived?
It's a 49-year-old female reported as unconscious, no known prior medical history.
RPD secured the scene. How did the patient appear on arrival, firefighter?
Lying in alley, breathing agonal, pinpoint pupils, no visible injuries.
Suspected opiate overdose - likely based on civilian Narcan dispenser found by firefighters.
Eyes aren't opening. Pupils are 1mm and non-reactive.
No verbal response to commands. GCS is 5 (Eye 1, Verbal 2, Motor 2).
Only pain response. Can't assess for spontaneous movement.
Airway is patent. Breathing - shallow and irregular, likely snoring respirations.
Will assist breathing with BVM. Confirm airway remains unobstructed during process.
Positive airway maintenance. Performing assisted ventilation now with BVM.
Respiratory rate appears agonal, approximately 4-6 per minute.
Now bagging at 10-12 breaths per minute, using 25 LPM oxygen.
Note slight chest rise and improvement of capnography as we increase rate.
Current oxygen saturation at 95%. Proceeding with nasal capnography setup.
Strong radial pulses present. Circulation is intact with no major wounds.
Heart rate is 99 beats per minute on time stamp 19:49.
Respiratory rate recorded at 8 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure reads 84/67.
Glucose is 86 mg/dL. No history of hypoglycemia.
Oxygen saturation 95%, stable with assisted breathing.
EKG showing paced rhythm from 19:49 readings.
Patient remains unresponsive. GCS 5 with positive response to painful stimuli.
Found evidence of prior tracheotomy and possible PIC line - no clear PMH from scene.
Narcan dispenser and no other bystanders suggests recreational opiate use likely.
No medications observed. IV access not successful.
No allergies noted. Unable to confirm through patient or family.
Confirmed: 49-year-old female weighing 82 kg.
Administering intranasal Naloxone 2 mg - per protocol for suspected opioid overdose.
Patient briefly began spontaneous breathing after Naloxone, but remains unresponsive.
Initiated IV access attempt in extremities. First IV start (20G needle) unsuccessful.
Second IV attempt also failed; going with IM route per standing order.
Heart rate now 94 beats per minute at 20:00 timestamp.
Respiratory rate reduced to 7 breaths per minute.
Rechecking BP, now elevated to 109/75.
Unable to secure new glucose reading. Set at 0 for now.
Oxygen saturation up to 99%.
EKG remains paced, no arrhythmias detected from 20:00 data point.
She started convulsing. Administering Midazolam 5 milligrams via intramuscular injection.
IM route successful. No clonus or shivering noted post-dose.
Placed 4-lead monitor, then completed the 12-lead EKG as per protocol.
DCAP-BTLS triangle: No injury indicators found. No penetrating trauma or contusions.
Skin is intact, warm, dry with no evidence of cyanosis or pallor.
Pupils remain 1mm, bilateral, and non-reactive. No visual abnormalities.
Equal bilateral breath sounds with mild decrease compared to norm.
Peripheral pulses stay strong, consistent with radial and pedal.
No visible deformity. Seizures affect evaluation of full motor range.
No abnormal skin color or petechiae. Jaundice not evident.
Foaming at the mouth noted during convulsions. No nasal drainage.
No signs of head injury. Comatose patient likely not experiencing neck pain.
Patient found in a non-emergent condition, but status upgraded to emergent due to sudden seizure.
Transport destination remains VCU ER. Upgraded to emergent status due to seizure.
Stretcher engagement set. All straps fastened and system ready.
Installed into ambulance - monitoring includes BVM and capnography.
Awaiting transfer to Green ER Room 3 at VCU Medical Center.
Hospital receiving notification. ED coordination confirmed.
En-route monitoring progressing with full-line equipment.
Continuing with nasal oxygen and capnography for airway management.
Midazolam (Versed) fails to terminate the convulsions. Noting full body extension.
Clenched fists observed during ongoing seizures.
Heart rate is 94 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is consistent at 7 per minute.
Blood pressure stands at 109/75 - trending upward.
EKG remains in normal sinus rhythm as of 20:04.
Grand-mal type seizure with flexed extremities and jaw clonus.
Based on naloxone dispenser and no prior medications, opiate use is suspected.
Arrival at hospital complex with green pathway from emergency department.
Transfer to negative pressure bed with assistance from nurse team.
Handover successfully executed. Patient under hospital care.
All logs, including Narcan use and Midazolam tempo, passed forward.
Convulsions ongoing - unable to confirm glucose levels. Set at 0 for now.
12-lead EKG consistent - ventricular pacing pattern observed.
Patient under medical protocol 7-1, arriving in active convulsion. Ready for ER intervention.
12-lead obtained with no ACS or STEMI exceeding 2 mm. Confirmed for cold to hot transition.
Oxygen adjusted to nasal using 25 LPM. Patient under stable to watchful emergency condition.
All item vitals, including pain at 0, safely transmitted for hospital handoff.
All EPG information processed and documented per protocol. Final handoff now complete.
Negative pressure room and ER green tag agree - emergency patient expected at block 3.
Full details and monitoring values belong to facilities. No leading concerns upon receipt.
Patient under care of ER nurse. EMT-B irrigation protocol ended.
All documentation and verbal tools included in handover - complete under protocol.
Route traversal time recorded as open and incident-free.
No further monitoring needed during handover - nursing assigned.
Patient transfer acceptable. No complications during current shift.
All parties fulfilled under medical protocol. Patient readiness confirmed.
No other indications for trauma or field interventions expected from this call.
All GCS and airway components stable for closing.
System engagement closed at ER transfer.
Post-assessment monitoring complete. No residual effects flagged in report.
Patient under standard hospital intake process.
Report signed and stamped. All parties cleared per hospital regulations.
Team departure initiated.
